bio.extra_names: Middle name honors maternal Carroll-of-Carrollton descent (via mother I01137)

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family burial ground.

education.notes: Bachelor's degree at Bryn Mawr — among the first generation of women to study at a peer-ranked women's college. (INST not built — single tree connection, though future stages may add the Helen Herron Taft Manning + Alice Talcott Enders + others connections.)
Master's degree in history at the University of Pennsylvania.

career.notes: Taught history at Miss Irwin's School, a Philadelphia girls' school.
Served on the board of the Prisoners Aid Society — among the rare upper-class civic associations focused on prison reform during her era.

Organized the Graeme Turnbull lecture series at La Paix — named for her husband's brother who died young. Brought T.S. Eliot, W.H. Auden, Robert Frost, Archibald MacLeish, and Marianne Moore to the Turnbull family estate to lecture.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived at the Turnbull family estate in Towson and hosted the Graeme Turnbull lectures there.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors maternal descent from Charles Carroll of Carrollton — Maryland's Catholic signer of the Declaration of Independence — through her mother Harriet Sterett Winchester Jones.

cross_connections.display_label: Same-generation Bryn Mawr history scholar; daughter of President Taft

narrative_blocks.header: Brought Eliot, Auden, Frost as Graeme Turnbull lecturers
narrative_blocks.body: Organized the Graeme Turnbull lecture series at La Paix — named for her husband's brother who died young — bringing T.S. Eliot, W.H. Auden, Robert Frost, Archibald MacLeish, and Marianne Moore to the Towson estate to lecture. The poets visited the same grounds where F. Scott and Zelda Fitzgerald had lived in a rented cottage a decade earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Bryn Mawr graduate seven years before women voted
narrative_blocks.body: Graduated Bryn Mawr more than a decade before the Nineteenth Amendment, then took a master's degree in history at the University of Pennsylvania. Taught history at Miss Irwin's School in Philadelphia and served on the board of the Prisoners Aid Society — all credentials uncommon for women of her generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her painter sister, her son, her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Lived to age 94 — outliving her elder sister Elizabeth the painter by thirteen years, her husband Bayard by twenty-seven, and her son Andrew the Fitzgerald biographer by eleven. The longevity preserved the La Paix tradition until the property was razed in 1962, after which she lived quietly in Maryland.

Auden, Robert Frost, Archibald MacLeish, and Marianne Moore to the Towson estate to lecture. The poets visited the same grounds where F. Scott and Zelda Fitzgerald had lived in a rented cottage a decade earlier.

narrative_blocks.body: Graduated Bryn Mawr more than a decade before the Nineteenth Amendment, then took a master's degree in history at the University of Pennsylvania. Taught history at Miss Irwin's School in Philadelphia and served on the board of the Prisoners Aid Society — all credentials uncommon for women of her generation.

The longevity preserved the La Paix tradition until the property was razed in 1962, after which she lived quietly in Maryland.

death.cause: Stricken at a French teachers meeting at Johns Hopkins University 1954.

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD.

parents.father_research_notes: Lawrence Turnbull built La Paix (LM111) as the family summer home 1885 in Towson MD. Not built in tree. Bayard's brother Graeme died young; Margaret's Graeme Turnbull lecture series named for him.

education.notes: Architectural training at the École des Beaux-Arts, Sorbonne, Paris — the gold-standard architectural training of his generation.

career.notes: Designed multiple houses for the extended Turnbull family in Baltimore: Grace Turnbull's residence at 223 Chancery Road, and houses at 4101 and 4105 Greenway. Major commission: 1919-1920 renovation of the 1719 main house at Richland Farm in Clarksville MD, including the colonnade of 22 Doric columns that defines the property. Richland Farm was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2008.
Taught French at the United States Naval Academy after his Sorbonne training.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived at the Turnbull family estate inherited from his father Lawrence Turnbull who built it in 1885.

cross_connections.display_label: Same-era Paris Beaux-Arts architect designing American family houses
Paris Prize 1912 ninth recipient; Penn Christian Association architect

narrative_blocks.header: Renovated Richland Farm 1919 with twenty-two Doric columns
narrative_blocks.body: Renovated the 1719 main house at Richland Farm in Clarksville Maryland 1919-1920, adding the colonnade of twenty-two Doric columns that defines the property today. Richland Farm was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2008.

narrative_blocks.header: Stricken at French teachers meeting, Johns Hopkins 1954
narrative_blocks.body: Collapsed at a meeting of French teachers held at Johns Hopkins University in 1954 — he had taught French at the United States Naval Academy after his École des Beaux-Arts training at the Sorbonne. The trained-architect-turned-French-teacher trajectory was itself unusual for a Beaux-Arts graduate of his era.

narrative_blocks.header: Beaux-Arts trained Sorbonne; designed Baltimore family houses
narrative_blocks.body: Earned his architectural training at the École des Beaux-Arts at the Sorbonne in Paris. Designed Grace Turnbull's residence at 223 Chancery Road in Baltimore plus the family houses at 4101 and 4105 Greenway, the work synthesizing his Paris classical training with the regional Maryland colonial idiom.

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family plot.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up at the Turnbull family estate in Towson, MD.

parents.father_research_notes: Per Johns Hopkins Magazine Oct 1986 obituary, direct descendant of Francis Scott Key (author Star-Spangled Banner) and Gen John Eager Howard (Maryland Revolutionary commander). Specific Kidder generational chain to Key + Howard not yet researched.

career.notes: Administrative supervisor at BG&E. Career documented in Johns Hopkins Magazine October 1986 obituary.

narrative_blocks.header: Francis Scott Key and John Eager Howard descendant
narrative_blocks.body: Direct descendant of Francis Scott Key — author of the Star-Spangled Banner — and Gen John Eager Howard, Maryland Revolutionary commander and 5th Governor of Maryland. Both descents documented in the Johns Hopkins Magazine October 1986 obituary.

death.cause: Suicide by carbon monoxide poisoning in closed garage at Brattle Street home.

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family plot.

notable.notable_blurb: Definitive biographer of F. Scott Fitzgerald, 1962

education.notes: St. Andrew's School, Middletown DE — boarding prep school.
Princeton University class of 1942. Colonial Club member.
Harvard University Ph.D. in European History 1954.

career.notes: Marshall Plan implementation staff at ECA Paris post-WWII before returning to Harvard for PhD.
MIT humanities instructor 1954-1958.

1956 New Yorker essays on F. Scott Fitzgerald at La Paix. 'Scott Fitzgerald' (Scribners 1962) — AP-praised as 'the best biography of the novelist.' 'The Letters of F. Scott Fitzgerald' (1963). 'Thomas Wolfe' (1968) — written under a Guggenheim Fellowship awarded 1964.
Brown University visiting professor 1969-1970; suicide came in his first academic year there.

military_service.unit: United States Naval Reserve, USS Palmer (DMS-5)
military_service.notes: Naval Reserve officer aboard USS Palmer (DMS-5, destroyer minesweeper) when ship was sunk by kamikaze attack at Lingayen Gulf January 7, 1945 — 28 crew killed, the rest evacuated.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up at the Turnbull family estate in Towson MD where he met F. Scott Fitzgerald age 11 in 1932.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Winchester' honors maternal grandmother Harriet Sterett Winchester Jones — the Charles Carroll of Carrollton descent line.

cross_connections.display_label: Harvard-trained editor of major American writer's letters
Senior ECA Paris diplomat on Marshall Plan implementation France

narrative_blocks.header: Met Fitzgerald age eleven at La Paix
narrative_blocks.body: Met F. Scott Fitzgerald in 1932 at age eleven when the Fitzgeralds rented a cottage on the La Paix estate where his family lived. The encounter became a literary friendship that produced two 1956 New Yorker essays and the 1962 Scribners biography 'Scott Fitzgerald.'

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote 'the best biography of the novelist', 1962
narrative_blocks.body: His 'Scott Fitzgerald' (Scribners 1962) was praised by the Associated Press as 'the best biography of the novelist.' Followed by 'The Letters of F. Scott Fitzgerald' (1963) and 'Thomas Wolfe' (1968, written under a 1964 Guggenheim Fellowship).

narrative_blocks.header: USS Palmer sank under him at Lingayen, 1945
narrative_blocks.body: Naval Reserve officer aboard the destroyer minesweeper USS Palmer when a kamikaze attack sank her at Lingayen Gulf January 7, 1945 — twenty-eight crew killed, the rest evacuated. After the war served ECA Paris on Marshall Plan implementation before returning to Harvard for the European-history doctorate.

narrative_blocks.header: Suicide by carbon monoxide on Brattle Street, 1970
narrative_blocks.body: Died by suicide in a closed garage at his Brattle Street home in Cambridge Massachusetts January 10, 1970, age 48 — shortly into his Brown University visiting professorship. Survived by wife Joanne Johnson and two daughters; no public note explaining the act.

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up at the Turnbull family estate in Towson MD.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Sterett' honors maternal grandmother Harriet Sterett Winchester Jones.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Stockton (Andrade) had a half-son Eduardo Andrade from a prior relationship.

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family plot.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Sterett' carries forward via his mother's middle name from great-grandmother Harriet Sterett Winchester Jones.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Founding minister of Brown Memorial, Baltimore

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family burial ground for Sparhawk-Jones / Turnbull / Kidder descendants.

career.notes: founding pastor
later pastorate

cross_connections.display_label: Pastor Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia 1838-1861; theological author

narrative_blocks.header: Founding minister of Baltimore's Brown Memorial Presbyterian
narrative_blocks.body: Pastored Brown Memorial Presbyterian Church Baltimore — the Bolton Hill congregation built around the memorial endowment for George Brown, the Baltimore-Ohio Railroad co-founder. Taught history and was an early advocate for women's higher education before being called to Calvary Church Philadelphia.

narrative_blocks.header: The only child of five to survive
narrative_blocks.body: Mayor Joel Jones of Philadelphia lost four children young in the 1830s and ‘40s; this son alone survived to adulthood, and became a minister. He founded Baltimore’s Brown Memorial Presbyterian, endowed for a B&O Railroad founder.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached women’s higher education
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond the pulpit he taught history and argued for women’s right to it — a Presbyterian minister advocating their higher education when few did. His own daughter Elizabeth became a noted painter.

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD.

parents.father_research_notes: Direct descendant of Charles Carroll of Carrollton (1737-1832, Maryland's Catholic signer of the Declaration of Independence and last surviving signer). Specific genealogical chain Carroll → Winchester → Jones not yet built in tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Carroll-of-Carrollton descent passed through her name
narrative_blocks.body: Direct descendant of Charles Carroll of Carrollton — Maryland's Catholic signer of the Declaration of Independence and its last surviving signer. The Carroll name passed to her younger daughter Margaret as her middle name, carrying the maternal Founder heritage into the twentieth-century Turnbull-Kidder Baltimore branch.

bio.title: Judge
bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT.

birth.notes: Wikipedia + biographical dictionary give Oct 26, 1795; FindAGrave gives Oct 25, 1795 — minor discrepancy. Using Wikipedia/canonical Oct 26.

marriages.notes: Per Wikipedia: 'June 14, 1831 married Eliza P. Sparhawk in Philadelphia, with whom he had six children.' Per FindAGrave 5 children named: Rev John Sparhawk Jones (TD0240 1841-1910 — built next stage); Elizabeth Jones (d.1837 young); Joel Jones (d.1837 young); Mary Jones (d.1844 young); Sarah Jones (d.1849 young). The Wikipedia '6 children' may include an unnamed sixth or a Rev John discrepancy. Only Rev John Sparhawk Jones survived to adulthood per all accounts.

education.notes: Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — INST022 in tree.

education.school_name: Apprenticeship with Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT
education.notes: Studied law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT after Yale.
Litchfield Law School 1819 under Tapping Reeve — completed legal training before being admitted to the bar in New Haven.

career.notes: Conducted an academy at Wilkes-Barre PA for a year and a half starting 1822 — after family crisis prompted his relocation from New Haven CT.
Practiced law at Easton PA for many years before relocating to Philadelphia.

Secretary to Lafayette College board of trustees 1826-1835; continued as trustee through 1852. (Lafayette College not yet built as INST — pending Kirkpatrick line stage which will add 4+ connections including grandnephew William Sebring Kirkpatrick.)

1830 appointed by Governor George Wolf as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the civil code of Pennsylvania. Published 1834 'A Syllabus of the Law of Land Office Titles in Pennsylvania.' Report submitted to PA Senate January 15, 1836.

Associate Judge Philadelphia District Court 1835-1845; Presiding Judge 1845-1847. Resigned 1847 to accept Girard College presidency. Notable Delaware River fisheries ruling upholding 1783 PA-NJ compact + private fishery rights as protected property despite federal navigation/commerce supremacy.

First president of Girard College 1847-1849. Girard's 1831 will founded the college for 'poor white male orphans' with controversial clause barring clergymen from campus (upheld by SCOTUS in Vidal v. Girard's Executors 1844).

Mayor of Philadelphia October 16, 1849 to October 15, 1850. Elected as Independent (some sources Democratic) candidate October 9, 1849 narrowly defeating Whig Charles Gilpin. Lost 1850 reelection to same Gilpin. Single-year term under pre-1854 city charter.
Elected member American Philosophical Society 1848. (APS not yet built as INST.)

Ruling elder Presbyterian Church. Theological writings: Notes on Scripture (1860 posthumous, published by widow); Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age (Girard College student use); Knowledge of One Another in the Future State; edited The Literalist (5 vols 8vo prophetic literature with original additions); translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome and of the Temporal Power of the Popes' from French. Advocate of literal Second Coming prophecy.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College B.A. 1817 with high honor.

notable.notable_blurb: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, Girard College first president

cross_connections.display_label: Federal Judge Eastern District PA; great-grandnephew via brother Matthew

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, narrow Independent victory
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Mayor of Philadelphia October 9, 1849 as the Independent (or Democratic) candidate, narrowly defeating Whig attorney Charles Gilpin to break the Whig Party's longstanding hold on the executive office. Inaugurated October 16, 1849 under the pre-1854 single-year charter. Lost his 1850 reelection bid to the same Gilpin amid the city's nativist Whig-Democrat ferment that would later fuel the Know-Nothing movement.

narrative_blocks.header: First president of Girard College, 1847-1849
narrative_blocks.body: Resigned the presiding judgeship of Philadelphia District Court 1847 to become the first president of Girard College — Stephen Girard's 1831 endowed institution for 'poor white male orphans.' Served two years amid lingering controversy from the Supreme Court's 1844 Vidal v. Girard's Executors decision upholding Girard's will clause barring clergymen from campus. Stepped down 1849 to enter the mayoral race.

narrative_blocks.header: Commissioned to revise Pennsylvania's civil code, 1830
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed by Governor George Wolf in 1830 as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the Commonwealth's civil code. The team's report to the Pennsylvania Senate January 15, 1836 produced enduring statutory compilations.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1817 high honor; Litchfield Law 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — the eldest of nine Coventry CT children. Apprenticed in law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven 1817-1819 before completing at Tapping Reeve's Litchfield Law School 1819. Admitted to the New Haven bar before family crisis prompted his relocation to Pennsylvania, where he conducted a Wilkes-Barre academy 1822-1823 and then practiced law at Easton PA for over a decade.

narrative_blocks.header: Scholar of prophecy and literal Second Coming
narrative_blocks.body: Ruling elder of the Presbyterian Church, with theological writings including the posthumous 'Notes on Scripture' (1860) and 'Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age' for Girard College students. Edited five volumes of English prophetic literature as 'The Literalist' with extensive original additions, advocating literal interpretation of scriptures predicting the Second Coming of Christ — and translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome' from the French.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT (Amasa's father). Joel Sr not built as separate entry — only known through Amasa's parentage.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Smith Sparhawk (?-1838), second wife of Dr John Sparhawk X02863. Marriage record not located. Not built. M1 was Aletheia Webster (1779).

INST143.name: University of Pennsylvania
INST143.notes:
Founded 1740 Philadelphia by Benjamin Franklin. Private Ivy League research university. Schools of particular tree relevance: Perelman School of Medicine (1765, oldest medical school in US), Wharton School of Business (1881, first collegiate business school in US), Stuart Weitzman School of Design (architecture).

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 13 across Hooker, Talcott, and spouse branches:

PACKARD MEDICAL DYNASTY (4 generations of physicians):
- H04335 Dr. Frederic Adolphus Packard (Penn Med)
- H02745 Dr. John Hooker Packard (Penn Med)
- H04337 Dr. Francis Randolph Packard (Penn Med)
- H04334 Charles Stuart Wood Packard

KIRKPATRICK BRANCH (Talcott line gen 8-10, three Lafayette-to-Penn transition figures):
- TD0249 William Huntington Kirkpatrick (Penn Law — also Lafayette A.B. 1905)
- TD0250 Donald Morris Kirkpatrick (Penn BS Architecture 1911 + Penn Christian Association leadership 1927-29 + Penn faculty 1931-34 + Paris Prize 1912 ninth recipient)
- TD0251 Miles Wells Kirkpatrick (Penn Law — Princeton BA + Penn Law; later ABA Antitrust Chair + FTC Chairman 1970-73)

TURNBULL BRANCH (Talcott gen 9-10):
- TD0242 Margaret Carroll Jones Turnbull (Penn)
- TD0248 Elizabeth Huntington Jones Kirkpatrick (Penn Christian Association connection via husband I01140 William Sebring + son TD0250 Donald)

OTHER:
- HD3384 Michael Gay Hooker
- TD0115 Michael Page (Wharton School of Business)
- X00641 Jonathan Huntington Lyman Jr. (Penn Med)
- X02844 Bancel La Farge (Penn Med)

Penn's location in Philadelphia made it the natural medical-school destination for both the Hooker-Packard physicians and the Jones-Kirkpatrick lawyers who moved to Philadelphia after Joel Jones's 1834 relocation from Easton.

[MERGED INST156 -> INST143, Phase 1 dedup]

INST114.name: United States Naval Academy
INST114.notes: Founded 1845 at Annapolis Maryland. Hooker-tree connections through Adm. George Dewey (TD0141, Acting Midshipman September 23, 1854, graduated June 18, 1858 fifth of fourteen; instructor 1868-1870; commanded USS Constitution as training ship 1867-1870; laid Memorial Chapel cornerstone June 1904) and his nephew Cdr. Theodore Gibbs Dewey (TD0150, graduate). Dewey Hall and Dewey Field at the Academy named for the Admiral. Significant beneficiary of George Goodwin Dewey's 1963 estate.

INST147.name: Brown Memorial Presbyterian Church
INST147.notes:
Founded 1870 Baltimore MD as a memorial to George Brown of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad (B&O), the first chartered railroad in the United States. Designed by architect Nathan Starkweather. One of the principal Old School Presbyterian congregations of Baltimore in the post-Civil War era.

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 7, all members of the extended Jones-Sparhawk family cluster centered around founding pastor TD0240 Rev. John Sparhawk Jones:

FOUNDING PASTOR:
- TD0240 Rev. John Sparhawk Jones (1841-1910, Talcott gen 8; founding pastor 1870+ before later moving to Calvary Presbyterian Philadelphia; author of religious books)

JOHN SPARHAWK JONES'S NUCLEAR FAMILY:
- I01137 Harriet Sterett Winchester Jones (wife)
- TD0241 Elizabeth Sparhawk-Jones (daughter, 1885-1968, the PAFA painter)

JOHN SPARHAWK JONES'S EXTENDED JONES FAMILY:
- I01136 Elizabeth Perkins Sparhawk Jones (mother of John Sparhawk Jones via TD0239 Joel)
- TD0242 Margaret Carroll Jones Turnbull (John's niece)
- TD0247 Matthew Hale Jones (John's uncle)
- TD0255 Rev. Joseph Huntington Jones DD (John's uncle — earlier Presbyterian pastor at Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia INST148)

Brown Memorial functioned as the Jones-Sparhawk family church across roughly two generations (1870-1910s) — a Baltimore counterpart to the Philadelphia Sixth Presbyterian (INST148) where the family's earlier generation had worshipped under Joseph H. Jones DD.

crossConnections.display_label: Same-generation Bryn Mawr history scholar; daughter of President Taft
